attributes.stem: A 60-year-old man is being assessed for refractory gastro-oesophageal reflux disease. He has been compliant in avoiding triggers, modifying his diet and working towards losing weight. His BMI is still 29. In conjunction with further lifestyle modifications, he is being maintained on omeprazole (20mg daily) which has given him some symptomatic relief. A surveillance endoscopy shows two areas of Barrett's extending 4 cm above the squamo-columnar junction. Biopsies of these areas confirms the presence of columnar epithelium with some high grade dysplasia.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
options: Active surveillance, repeat endoscopy in 3-6 months
Active surveillance, repeat endoscopy in 3-5 years
attributes.explanation:
This patient has Barrett's oesophagus with high-grade dysplasia, which carries a significant risk of progression to adenocarcinoma (approximately 7% per year). Given this high risk, prompt endoscopic intervention is recommended rather than further surveillance.

Radiofrequency ablation is the most appropriate management option. It is a form of endoscopic eradication therapy that involves controlled thermal ablation of the Barrett's mucosa, allowing regeneration with normal squamous epithelium. It is effective and has a low rate of complications, although may lead to adverse events including stricture formation.

The management approach to Barrett's oesophagus is influenced by patient factors and disease factors including the presence of dysplasia or visible mucosal irregularities. While there is some variation between guidelines, most recent recommendations are as follows:

  • Non-dysplastic Barrett's: endoscopic surveillance every 3-5 years depending on the length of the Barrett's segment

  • Indefinite for dysplasia: optimise medical reflux therapy and repeat endoscopy within 6 months

  • Low-grade dysplasia: either endoscopic eradication therapy or 6-monthly surveillance

  • High-grade dysplasia and early intramucosal adenocarcinoma: endoscopic eradication therapy, which may involve a combination of:

    • Ablative therapy (i.e. radiofrequency ablation) if no mucosal abnormalities are present

    • Endoscopic resection (i.e. endoscopic mucosal resection or endoscopic submucosal dissection) of mucosal irregularities - this is both therapeutic and enables histological assessment of the resected specimen

    • If there is evidence of adenocarcinoma invasion beyond the submucosa, staging investigations and referral to an oncology centre

  • Visible mucosal irregularities should undergo endoscopic resection and histological examination (see high-grade dysplasia)

All patients should continue proton pump inhibitor therapy and lifestyle modifications regardless of the degree of dysplasia. Dysplasia should be confirmed by an expert pathologist and be managed in a specialised centre.

Fundoplication surgery addresses reflux but does not treat the dysplastic Barrett's mucosa. While it may be considered for refractory reflux symptoms, it would not address the immediate concern of high-grade dysplasia.

Increasing omeprazole dosage might help control reflux symptoms but would not prevent progression of dysplasia to cancer. Proton pump inhibitors are used as adjunctive therapy but not as primary management of dysplasia.

Active surveillance every 3-6 months could be considered for low-grade dysplasia, where the annual risk of progression to cancer is much lower (0.5%). However, it is inadequate for high-grade dysplasia given the higher risk of progression.

Active surveillance every 3-5 years is the recommended interval for non-dysplastic Barrett's oesophagus only.
learningPoints: Barrett's oesophagus with high-grade dysplasia has a 7% annual risk of progression to adenocarcinoma
Endoscopic eradication therapies including radiofrequency ablation and endoscopic mucosal resection recommended for the management of Barrett's with high-grade dysplasia.
Management approaches for Barrett's oesophagus depend on the presence and grade of dysplasia and the presence of mucosal irregularities
references: Shaheen NJ, Falk GW, Iyer PG, et al. Diagnosis and Management of Barrett's Esophagus: An Updated ACG Guideline. Am J Gastroenterol. 2022;117(4):559-587. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259184/

Weusten BLAM, Bisschops R, Dinis-Ribeiro M, et al. Diagnosis and management of Barrett esophagus: European Society of Gastrointestinal Endoscopy (ESGE) Guideline. Endoscopy. 2023;55(12):1124-1146. Available at: https://www.thieme-connect.com/products/ejournals/abstract/10.1055/a-2176-2440
